engineIntelligence.h2: Suzuki Swift Engines - Codes, Common Failures & Replacement Costs
engineIntelligence.description: The cards below cover the main engines fitted to the Suzuki Swift in the UK, including documented failure causes, variant production years, and typical rebuilt engine costs based on current UK market data.

cards.altText: Suzuki Swift K12B engine - petrol 1.2L replacement UK
knownFailures: Timing chain tensioner wear - rattle on cold start after 80k miles, documented TSB.
Head gasket failure - overheating issues reported on 2012 model year units.
Coil pack degradation - misfires and rough idle on higher-mileage examples.

cards.altText: Suzuki Swift M13A engine - petrol 1.3L replacement UK
knownFailures: Timing chain tensioner wear - cold-start rattle common after 100k miles on early builds.
EGR valve coking - rough idle and emissions test failures in urban-driven examples.
Intake manifold gasket leaks - vacuum leaks causing lean misfires on aged units.

cards.altText: Suzuki Swift M16A engine - petrol 1.6L replacement UK
knownFailures: Oil consumption increase - piston ring wear reported on early-production Sport units.
Timing chain stretch - metallic rattle on cold start after high-mileage use.
Coil pack failure - misfires under load on higher-mileage enthusiast-driven examples.

commonProblems.h2: Suzuki Swift Engine Problems - What It Costs to Fix & When Replacement Makes Sense
commonProblems.h3: The Suzuki Swift's K-series and M-series petrol engines are generally robust, but timing chain tensioner wear (M13A/K12B) and head gasket issues (2012 K12B models) drive most replacement enquiries. Every rebuilt engine supplied through EngineMarket includes upgraded internal components designed to address the original failure point - plus a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty.

problems.h4: Timing Chain Tensioner Failure - Why K-Series Swift Engines Rattle on Cold Start
problems.affectedModels: Suzuki Swift GL, GLS, SZ3, SZ-L (2005-2017, M13A & K12B engines)
problems.rootCause: Hydraulic tensioner degradation combined with extended oil intervals causes chain slack and cold-start rattle; documented in Suzuki TSB for early K-series builds.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace tensioner and guide rail; reset cam timing
repairOptions.longevity: Viable only before catastrophic chain jump; best caught early

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Supply & fit rebuilt K12B/M13A with upgraded tensioner, bench-tested, 12-month warranty
repairOptions.longevity: Best value for high-mileage Swifts; eliminates chain risk entirely

problems.recommendation: If your Swift has under 90k miles and the rattle is mild, a preventative tensioner replacement from a specialist offers good value. For engines over 100k miles or with metallic debris in the oil, a rebuilt unit is the safer long-term choice.
problems.cta: Compare rebuilt K12B engine prices for your Suzuki Swift

problems.h4: Head Gasket Failure - Why 2012 Swift 1.2 Engines Overheat
problems.affectedModels: Suzuki Swift SZ3, SZ-L, SZ-T (2012 model year, K12B engine)
problems.rootCause: Manufacturing variance in head gasket sealing on 2012 K12B units; overheating or coolant loss leads to warping and compression loss.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Remove cylinder head, replace gasket, resurface if needed, pressure test
repairOptions.longevity: Viable if block is undamaged; requires skilled machining

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Supply & fit rebuilt K12B with upgraded gasket set, bench-tested, 12-month warranty
repairOptions.longevity: Best for high-mileage or warped-block examples; eliminates recurrence risk

problems.recommendation: If the block is undamaged and mileage is under 85k, a head gasket replacement can be cost-effective. For engines with visible warping, coolant contamination of oil, or over 100k miles, a rebuilt unit offers better long-term value.
problems.cta: Get quotes for K12B Suzuki Swift engine replacement

problems.h4: Oil Consumption Increase - Why Swift Sport M16A Engines Burn Oil
problems.rootCause: Piston ring wear on early-production M16A units, exacerbated by enthusiastic driving and infrequent oil changes; leads to blue exhaust smoke and low oil warnings.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Strip bottom end, replace rings and bearings, reassemble with new gaskets
repairOptions.longevity: Viable if cylinder bores are within spec; requires expert machining

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Supply & fit rebuilt M16A with upgraded rings, bench-tested, 12-month warranty
repairOptions.longevity: Best for high-mileage Sport models; eliminates oil consumption and restores performance

problems.recommendation: For Swift Sport owners who value performance and reliability, a rebuilt M16A engine is the most dependable solution. If the engine is under 100k miles and bores are good, ring replacement can work-but factor in the risk of future wear.
problems.cta: Compare rebuilt M16A Sport engine prices for your Suzuki Swift

problems.h4: Turbocharger Failure - Why Swift Sport Boosterjet K14C Loses Boost
problems.affectedModels: Suzuki Swift Sport Boosterjet (2017-present, K14C engine)
problems.rootCause: Wastegate actuator sticking and carbon buildup on turbo vanes; common on direct-injection turbo engines with short-trip urban use.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Clean turbo, replace wastegate actuator, reset ECU adaptations
repairOptions.longevity: Viable if turbo core is undamaged; may not resolve underlying carbon issues

repairOptions.tier: Full Replacement - Rebuilt Turbo or Engine
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Supply & fit rebuilt K14C engine or remanufactured turbo, bench-tested, 12-month warranty
repairOptions.longevity: Best for high-mileage or heavily carboned units; eliminates boost loss risk

problems.recommendation: If boost loss is mild and the turbo core is sound, a specialist turbo service can restore performance. For persistent limp mode, carboned intake valves, or over 100k miles, a rebuilt K14C engine offers the most reliable long-term fix.
problems.cta: Get quotes for K14C Boosterjet Suzuki Swift engine replacement

problems.h4: DPF Clogging - Why Swift 1.3 DDiS Diesel Engines Enter Limp Mode
problems.affectedModels: Suzuki Swift 1.3 DDiS (2007-2010, D13A Multijet engine)
problems.rootCause: Incomplete DPF regeneration cycles on short-trip urban driving; soot accumulation triggers limp mode and eventual blockage.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Remove DPF for off-car cleaning or perform forced regen via diagnostic tool
repairOptions.longevity: Viable if DPF is not physically damaged; requires honest driving cycle post-service

repairOptions.tier: Full Replacement - Rebuilt Engine or DPF
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Supply & fit rebuilt D13A engine or new DPF assembly, bench-tested, 12-month warranty
repairOptions.longevity: Best for high-mileage diesels or physically damaged DPFs; eliminates recurrence risk

problems.recommendation: If your Swift diesel is used for regular motorway runs, a DPF clean can restore function. For exclusively urban use or physically damaged DPFs, a rebuilt engine (or DPF replacement) is the more reliable long-term solution.
problems.cta: Compare rebuilt D13A diesel engine prices for your Suzuki Swift

finalCta.h4: Don't Let Engine Failure Write Off Your Suzuki Swift
finalCta.paragraph: Timing chain rattle, head gasket leaks, oil consumption, turbo failure, or DPF blockage-these common Swift engine problems don't have to mean a write-off. EngineMarket makes it simple to compare prices from trusted UK Suzuki Swift specialists for rebuilt, reconditioned or used engines. Every unit includes upgraded components addressing the original failure point, plus a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty and nationwide delivery. Compare prices today and get back on the road without the main dealer premium.
finalCta.buttonText: Compare Suzuki Swift Engine Replacement Prices Now →
finalCta.disclaimer: All prices are indicative UK market ranges based on historical quote data and repair requests from our network of engine specialists. Dealer pricing is estimated based on published main dealer labour rates and genuine OEM parts pricing. Specialist pricing reflects actual quote data from EngineMarket's UK network. Actual costs vary depending on damage severity, vehicle condition, variant, and workshop location within the UK. Use our free comparison tool to get tailored quotes for your specific Suzuki Swift and engine code. Every engine supplied through EngineMarket carries a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty - always confirm exact terms with your chosen supplier.

engineSizes.h2: Suzuki Swift Engines by Size - Find the Right Displacement for Your Variant
engineSizes.intro: Every displacement fitted to the Suzuki Swift is listed below with compatible engine codes, production years and common failure notes to help you identify the right replacement.

items.title: Suzuki Swift 1.2L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.2L K12B and K12C engines powered the majority of Swifts from 2010 onwards, offering an excellent balance of fuel economy and reliability for daily driving. The K12B (2010-2017) is the most common replacement request, while the later K12C (2017-present) adds mild-hybrid SHVS technology for improved efficiency.
commonFailurePoints: Timing chain tensioner wear on K12B; HPFP
VVT phaser issues on early K12C units
items.cta: Compare Suzuki Swift 1.2L petrol engine prices

items.title: Suzuki Swift 1.3L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.3L M13A was the standard engine for first-generation Swifts (2005-2010), delivering smooth, reliable performance for GL, GLS and GLX trims. While generally robust, early builds are prone to timing chain tensioner wear at higher mileage.
commonFailurePoints: Timing chain tensioner wear; EGR valve coking on urban-driven examples
items.cta: Compare Suzuki Swift 1.3L petrol engine prices

items.title: Suzuki Swift 1.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.5L M15A powered top-spec GLX and select export Swift variants from 2005-2010, offering 101hp for drivers wanting extra performance without stepping up to the Sport model.
commonFailurePoints: Timing chain tensioner wear; oil consumption on high-mileage enthusiast-driven units
items.cta: Compare Suzuki Swift 1.5L petrol engine prices

items.title: Suzuki Swift 1.6L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.6L M16A is the naturally aspirated heart of the 2006-2012 Swift Sport, delivering 136hp and linear power delivery beloved by enthusiasts. Oil consumption on early units is the most common replacement trigger.
commonFailurePoints: Oil consumption from piston ring wear; timing chain stretch on high-mileage examples
items.cta: Compare Suzuki Swift 1.6L petrol engine prices

items.title: Suzuki Swift 1.4L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.4L K14C Boosterjet turbo powers the 2017-present Swift Sport, delivering 138hp with modern efficiency. Turbo wastegate sticking and carbon buildup on intake valves are the primary concerns for replacement enquiries.
commonFailurePoints: Turbo wastegate sticking; carbon buildup on direct-injection intake valves
items.cta: Compare Suzuki Swift 1.4L petrol engine prices

items.title: Suzuki Swift 1.3L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 1.3L D13A Multijet diesel was offered on Swifts from 2007-2010, providing strong fuel economy for high-mileage drivers. DPF clogging on short-trip urban use is the most common failure leading to replacement enquiries.
commonFailurePoints: DPF clogging on short-trip use; EGR valve sticking; injector wear at high mileage
items.cta: Get quotes for Suzuki Swift 1.3L diesel engines

Swift Sport engines attract enthusiast driving, increasing wear risk

EGR valve sticking common on aged units
Lower replacement volume than petrol, but higher per-lead value
items.cta: Get quotes for Suzuki Swift Diesel replacement engines

engineYears.h2: Suzuki Swift Engines by Year - What Was Fitted & When
engineYears.intro: This section covers all major engine changes across the Suzuki Swift's UK production timeline, organised so owners can quickly identify their engine and compare rebuilt engine prices from UK specialists.

years.description: The global Swift launched in 2005 with M-series petrol engines, establishing the model's reputation for fun, reliable motoring.
keyChanges: M13A 1.3L petrol introduced as standard engine
M16A 1.6L launched for Swift Sport variant (2006)
knownFor: M13A praised for smoothness and reliability
Timing chain tensioner wear emerges on high-mileage early builds
Swift Sport M16A attracts enthusiast driving, increasing oil consumption reports
D13A diesel offers strong economy but DPF issues on urban use
years.cta: Get quotes for 2005-2010 Suzuki Swift replacement engines
years.ticker: Suzuki Swift M13A engine replacement • Swift Sport M16A oil consumption • Swift 1.3 DDiS DPF problems

years.description: The 2010 facelift introduced the K12B 1.2L engine, which became the dominant Swift powerplant for the next decade.
keyChanges: K12B 1.2L VVT replaces M13A as standard petrol engine
New SZ-L, SZ-T, SZ5 trim levels introduced
Improved interior and safety equipment across range
knownFor: K12B generally reliable but timing chain tensioner wear documented in TSB
Head gasket issues reported on 2012 model year K12B units
Swift Sport M16A oil consumption remains a concern for enthusiast owners
Strong parts availability supports affordable rebuilds
years.cta: Get quotes for 2010-2017 Suzuki Swift replacement engines
years.ticker: Suzuki Swift K12B timing chain • Swift 2012 head gasket failure • Swift Sport M16A rebuild cost

years.description: The 2017 Swift introduced SHVS mild-hybrid technology and the K14C Boosterjet turbo for the Sport, modernising the range.
keyChanges: K12C 1.2L Dualjet with SHVS mild-hybrid becomes standard
K14C 1.4L Boosterjet turbo launched for new Swift Sport
HEARTECT platform reduces weight, improves efficiency
Advanced safety tech added across trim levels
mainEngines: K12C - 1.2L Dualjet SHVS (SZ5 SHVS / Motion SHVS)
knownFor: K12C SHVS improves fuel economy but HPFP wear reported on early units
K14C Boosterjet delivers strong performance but turbo wastegate concerns
Mild-hybrid system requires specialist knowledge for engine replacement
Growing demand for rebuilt K12C and K14C units as models age
years.cta: Enquire about Suzuki Swift 2017+ replacement engines
years.ticker: Suzuki Swift K12C SHVS engine • Swift Sport Boosterjet turbo replacement • K14C HPFP failure cost

faq.h2: Suzuki Swift Engine Replacement - Frequently Asked Questions

items.question: How much does it cost to replace a Suzuki Swift engine in the UK?
items.answer: Rebuilt Suzuki Swift engines typically cost £850-£2,200 supply-only, depending on engine code and variant. K12B and M13A units are most affordable; Sport M16A and Boosterjet K14C command premium pricing.
keyPoints: K12B 1.2L rebuilt: £900-£1,600 supply only
M13A 1.3L rebuilt: £850-£1,500 supply only
M16A Sport rebuilt: £1,400-£2,200 supply only
Supply & fit adds £450-£850 labour depending on location
items.cta: Compare rebuilt Suzuki Swift engine prices for your variant

items.question: What engines fit a Suzuki Swift?
items.answer: Only engines officially fitted to your Swift generation are guaranteed compatible: M13A/M15A/M16A (2005-2010), K12B (2010-2017), K12C/K14C (2017-present). Cross-generation swaps require extensive modification.
keyPoints: Verify engine code via registration or VIN before ordering
K12B does not directly swap into pre-2010 Swifts without ECU work
Sport engines (M16A/K14C) require matching transmission and mounts
Use our registration lookup to confirm exact compatibility
items.cta: Get quotes for compatible Suzuki Swift engine replacement

items.question: Are Suzuki Swift engines reliable?
items.answer: Swift engines are generally reliable with proper maintenance, but K-series units (M13A/K12B) have documented timing chain tensioner wear, and 2012 K12B models had head gasket issues.
keyPoints: Regular oil changes critical for timing chain longevity
2012 K12B models warrant extra head gasket vigilance
Swift Sport engines endure harder use, increasing wear risk
Rebuilt units address known failure points for improved reliability
items.cta: Find a reconditioned Suzuki Swift engine with 12-month warranty

items.question: How long does a Suzuki Swift engine last?
items.answer: With proper maintenance, Swift engines commonly reach 150,000+ miles. Timing chain tensioner wear (80-120k miles) and head gasket issues (2012 models) are the most common premature failure points.
keyPoints: Oil changes every 10k miles or 12 months protect chain system
Address cold-start rattle promptly to avoid catastrophic failure
High-mileage engines benefit from rebuilt units with upgraded components
Service history significantly impacts longevity expectations
items.cta: Compare high-mileage Suzuki Swift engine replacement options

items.question: What causes Suzuki Swift timing chain rattle?
items.answer: Hydraulic timing chain tensioner wear on M13A and K12B engines causes cold-start rattle, typically after 80,000 miles. Ignoring it risks chain jump and catastrophic engine damage.
keyPoints: Rattle on cold start that fades when warm is classic tensioner symptom
Suzuki issued TSB for early K-series tensioner designs
Preventative replacement costs £280-£480 specialist; rebuilt engine £900-£1,600
Metallic debris in oil indicates advanced wear requiring full replacement
items.cta: Get quotes for Suzuki Swift timing chain repair or replacement

items.question: Is the Suzuki Swift Sport engine worth replacing?
items.answer: Yes-if you value the Sport's performance, a rebuilt M16A or K14C engine restores reliability at a fraction of new-car cost. Enthusiast demand keeps Sport engine values strong.
keyPoints: Upgraded components address oil consumption (M16A) or turbo issues (K14C)
12-month warranty protects your investment
Compare quotes to find best-value specialist for Sport engines
items.cta: Compare rebuilt Swift Sport engine prices from UK specialists

items.question: Can I put a Swift Sport engine in my standard Swift?
items.answer: Technically possible but complex and costly. M16A or K14C swaps require matching transmission, ECU, wiring, mounts and exhaust-often exceeding the cost of a rebuilt standard engine.
keyPoints: Engine swap requires significant fabrication and ECU reprogramming
Insurance and MOT implications must be considered
Rebuilt standard engine often better value than Sport conversion
Consult a specialist before committing to a swap project
items.cta: Enquire about Suzuki Swift engine compatibility and upgrade options

items.question: What is the warranty on a reconditioned Suzuki Swift engine?
items.answer: Engines supplied through EngineMarket include a minimum 12-month unlimited mileage warranty from vetted UK specialists. Always confirm exact terms with your chosen supplier before ordering.
keyPoints: Warranty covers parts and labour for manufacturing defects
Unlimited mileage terms vary by supplier-verify before purchase
Keep service records to maintain warranty validity
12-month coverage provides peace of mind for daily drivers
items.cta: Find Suzuki Swift engines with 12-month warranty coverage

items.question: How do I find my Suzuki Swift engine code?
items.answer: Your engine code is stamped on the block (visible near the gearbox) and listed on your V5C logbook under "Engine number". Enter your registration above for instant DVLA verification.
keyPoints: Common codes: M13A, K12B, M16A, K14C, D13A
Code location varies by generation-consult owner's manual
DVLA lookup via registration is fastest verification method
Correct code ensures you order the right replacement engine
items.cta: Identify your Suzuki Swift engine code instantly via registration

items.question: Is it worth repairing or replacing my Suzuki Swift engine?
items.answer: For engines over 100k miles or with catastrophic failures (chain jump, warped head), replacement with a rebuilt unit is usually better value than repair. For minor issues under 80k miles, repair may suffice.
keyPoints: Compare repair quote against rebuilt engine price for your variant
Rebuilt units include upgraded parts addressing original failure
Consider vehicle value, mileage and your ownership plans
Get multiple quotes via our comparison tool to decide confidently
items.cta: Compare repair vs replacement costs for your Suzuki Swift engine
